Outcomes.Text: Explain the purpose and structure of Python modules and how they are imported into scripts.
Create custom Python modules and reuse them across multiple engineering scripts.
Apply the with-statement context manager to safely read and write text and CSV files.
Demonstrate how to encode and decode data using Python's JSON module.
Identify common runtime errors in engineering scripts and handle them using try-except patterns.
Analyze standard library modules to select appropriate tools for common programming tasks.

Topics.Title: Introduction to Python Modules
Topics.Summary: Explains what modules are, why they are used, and how Python locates and loads them at runtime.

Elements.Title: What Is a Module?
Elements.BodyText: A module is a single Python file containing definitions, functions, classes, and variables that can be reused in other scripts.
Elements.Notes: Think of a module as a toolbox: you build the tools once and import them wherever needed, avoiding code duplication across engineering projects.

Items.Text: A module file uses the .py extension and can be stored anywhere on the Python path.
Modules promote code reuse, readability, and separation of concerns.
Any valid Python file can act as a module without additional configuration.

Elements.Title: Importing Modules with import
Elements.BodyText: The import statement loads a module into the current namespace, making its contents accessible via dot notation.
Elements.Notes: Using the module name as a prefix (e.g., math.sqrt) avoids name collisions when multiple modules define identically named functions.

Items.Text: Use 'import module_name' to load the entire module.
Access module contents with the syntax 'module_name.attribute'.
A module is executed only once per interpreter session, even if imported multiple times.

Elements.Title: Selective Imports with from…import
Elements.BodyText: The from…import syntax lets you import specific names from a module directly into the current namespace.
Elements.Notes: While convenient, wildcard imports (from module import *) can shadow existing names unexpectedly and are generally discouraged in production code.

Items.Text: Use 'from module import name' to bring a single attribute into scope.
Multiple names can be imported in one statement separated by commas.
Selective imports can improve readability but may obscure where a name originates.

Elements.Title: Module Aliases
Elements.BodyText: The 'as' keyword creates a shorter or more descriptive alias for an imported module or name.
Elements.Notes: Community-standard aliases such as 'import numpy as np' and 'import pandas as pd' improve code portability and readability across engineering teams.

Items.Text: Use 'import module_name as alias' to assign a shorthand reference.
Aliases are particularly useful for modules with long or frequently typed names.
The alias only exists in the current script's namespace.

Elements.Title: The Module Search Path
Elements.BodyText: When Python encounters an import statement it searches a sequence of directories defined in sys.path to locate the module file.
Elements.Notes: sys.path can be inspected and modified at runtime, allowing scripts to import modules from project-specific directories not on the default path.

Items.Text: Python searches the current directory first, then installed package directories.
The PYTHONPATH environment variable can add directories to sys.path.
If the module is not found in any path location, an ImportError is raised.

Topics.Title: Writing and Using Custom Modules
Topics.Summary: Covers how to design, write, and import your own Python modules to share reusable engineering functions across projects.

Elements.Title: Creating a Custom Module File
Elements.BodyText: A custom module is created by writing functions, classes, or constants in a .py file and saving it in a location accessible to your scripts.
Elements.Notes: Organizing related engineering utilities into a single module (e.g., unit_conversions.py) keeps projects tidy and makes testing individual functions straightforward.

Items.Text: Choose a descriptive, lowercase filename with underscores for readability.
Include a module-level docstring to describe the module's purpose.
Define all reusable functions and constants at the top level of the file.

Elements.Title: The __name__ == '__main__' Guard
Elements.BodyText: This guard allows a module file to contain both reusable code and a standalone test or demo block that only executes when the file is run directly.
Elements.Notes: Without this guard, any test code at the top level of your module would execute automatically whenever another script imports it, causing unintended side effects.

Items.Text: When a file is run directly, Python sets __name__ to '__main__'.
When a file is imported, __name__ is set to the module's filename without .py.
Place demonstration, testing, or script-entry logic inside the if __name__ == '__main__' block.

Elements.Title: Organizing Functions and Constants
Elements.BodyText: Group related functions and constants logically within a module to make the interface clear to other developers and scripts.
Elements.Notes: Using ALL_CAPS for module-level constants is a Python convention that signals the value should not be reassigned by importing code.

Items.Text: Place constants near the top of the module, before function definitions.
Write each function with a clear docstring describing inputs, outputs, and units where applicable.
Avoid defining mutable global state in a module to prevent unexpected side effects.

Elements.Title: Importing Your Custom Module
Elements.BodyText: Once saved in the correct directory, a custom module is imported using the same syntax as any standard library module.
Elements.Notes: If your custom module is in the same directory as the script that imports it, no path manipulation is needed. For larger projects, consider packaging modules into a subdirectory with an __init__.py file.

Items.Text: Ensure the module file is on the Python search path before importing.
Use 'import module_name' or 'from module_name import function' as needed.
Changes to the module file require a restart of the interpreter or use of importlib.reload to take effect.

Elements.Title: Documenting a Custom Module
Elements.BodyText: Good documentation within a module enables other engineers to understand and correctly use its functions without reading the implementation.
Elements.Notes: The built-in help() function displays a module's docstring and the docstrings of all its public functions, making in-line documentation immediately accessible.

Items.Text: Write a module-level docstring as the very first statement in the file.
Provide docstrings for every public function describing parameters, return values, and any raised exceptions.
Type hints in function signatures further clarify expected argument and return types.

Topics.Title: Using Standard Library Modules
Topics.Summary: Introduces key modules included with Python that are immediately useful for engineering scripting tasks without additional installation.

Elements.Title: The math Module
Elements.BodyText: The math module provides mathematical functions and constants commonly needed in engineering calculations.
Elements.Notes: math functions operate on scalar values. For array-based operations, NumPy's equivalents are preferred in engineering workflows.

Items.Text: Access constants such as math.pi and math.e for precise numeric values.
Use functions like math.sqrt, math.log, math.sin, and math.cos for common calculations.
math.degrees and math.radians convert between angle units.

Elements.Title: The os and os.path Modules
Elements.BodyText: The os module provides operating-system-independent functions for interacting with the file system and environment.
Elements.Notes: Using os.path.join to construct file paths instead of string concatenation ensures scripts run correctly on both Windows and Unix-based systems.

Items.Text: Use os.getcwd() to retrieve and os.chdir() to change the current working directory.
os.path.join constructs file paths in a platform-safe manner.
os.listdir returns the contents of a directory as a list of filenames.

Elements.Title: The sys Module
Elements.BodyText: The sys module exposes interpreter-level variables and functions, including command-line arguments and the module search path.
Elements.Notes: sys.argv is a list where sys.argv[0] is always the script name; additional elements are arguments passed by the user at the command line.

Items.Text: sys.argv provides a list of command-line arguments passed to a script.
sys.exit() terminates the interpreter with an optional status code.
sys.path is the list of directories Python searches when importing modules.

Elements.Title: The datetime Module
Elements.BodyText: The datetime module supplies classes for manipulating dates and times, which is useful for timestamping engineering data logs.
Elements.Notes: datetime.now() returns the local current date and time; datetime.utcnow() returns UTC time. For timezone-aware applications, consider the zoneinfo module introduced in Python 3.9.

Items.Text: Use datetime.datetime.now() to capture the current timestamp.
Arithmetic on datetime objects produces timedelta objects representing durations.
strftime formats a datetime object as a human-readable string using format codes.

Elements.Title: The random Module
Elements.BodyText: The random module generates pseudo-random numbers and provides tools for random sampling, useful in simulations and testing.
Elements.Notes: For reproducible results in testing or simulations, call random.seed() with a fixed integer before generating values.

Items.Text: random.random() returns a float uniformly distributed between 0.0 and 1.0.
random.randint(a, b) returns a random integer N such that a ≤ N ≤ b.
random.choice selects a random element from a non-empty sequence.

Topics.Title: File I/O with the with Statement
Topics.Summary: Explains how to open, read, and write text files safely using Python's with-statement context manager to guarantee proper resource cleanup.

Elements.Title: Opening Files and File Modes
Elements.BodyText: Python's built-in open() function returns a file object that provides methods for reading and writing; the mode argument determines how the file is accessed.
Elements.Notes: Common modes: 'r' (read, default), 'w' (write, truncates), 'a' (append), 'x' (exclusive creation). Add 'b' for binary mode, e.g., 'rb'.

Items.Text: The first argument to open() is the file path as a string or Path object.
The mode argument controls whether the file is opened for reading, writing, or appending.
An optional encoding argument (e.g., encoding='utf-8') prevents character-encoding errors on different platforms.

Elements.Title: The with Statement Context Manager
Elements.BodyText: The with statement ensures a file is automatically closed when the indented block exits, even if an exception occurs.
Elements.Notes: Without the with statement, a file must be closed explicitly with file.close(). Forgetting this can lead to data loss or resource leaks, especially in long-running engineering scripts.

Items.Text: Use 'with open(path, mode) as f:' to open a file within a managed context.
The file object is bound to the alias after 'as' and is available inside the block.
The file is closed automatically when the with block exits, regardless of errors.

Elements.Title: Reading Text Files
Elements.BodyText: File objects provide multiple methods for reading text content depending on whether you need the entire file, one line, or all lines as a list.
Elements.Notes: For large files, iterating over the file object line by line (for line in f) is more memory-efficient than reading the entire file into memory at once with read().

Items.Text: f.read() returns the entire file content as a single string.
f.readline() reads and returns one line at a time, including the newline character.
f.readlines() returns a list of all lines in the file.

Elements.Title: Writing Text Files
Elements.BodyText: Opening a file in write or append mode allows you to output strings to the file using the write or writelines methods.
Elements.Notes: f.write() does not add a newline automatically; you must include '\n' explicitly at the end of each line. Using mode 'w' overwrites an existing file completely.

Items.Text: f.write(string) writes the given string to the file and returns the number of characters written.
f.writelines(list) writes each item in a list to the file without adding separators.
Open in append mode ('a') to add content to an existing file without erasing it.

Elements.Title: Working with File Paths Using pathlib
Elements.BodyText: The pathlib module provides an object-oriented interface for constructing and manipulating file paths that works consistently across operating systems.
Elements.Notes: Path objects can be passed directly to open() instead of strings, and they offer convenient attributes like .stem, .suffix, .parent, and methods like .exists() and .mkdir().

Items.Text: Use Path from pathlib to create platform-independent path objects.
The / operator joins path components intuitively, e.g., Path('data') / 'results.txt'.
Path.exists() returns True if the path points to an existing file or directory.

Topics.Title: Reading and Writing CSV Files
Topics.Summary: Covers using Python's built-in csv module to read tabular data from CSV files and write structured results, a common task in engineering data workflows.

Elements.Title: What Is a CSV File?
Elements.BodyText: A CSV (Comma-Separated Values) file stores tabular data as plain text, with each row on a new line and columns separated by a delimiter, typically a comma.
Elements.Notes: CSV is widely used to exchange data between measurement instruments, spreadsheet software, and Python analysis scripts in engineering environments.

Items.Text: The first row of a CSV file often contains column headers.
Fields containing the delimiter or newlines are enclosed in quotation marks.
The delimiter can be customized; tabs, semicolons, and pipes are common alternatives.

Elements.Title: Reading CSV Files with csv.reader
Elements.BodyText: csv.reader wraps a file object and returns an iterator that yields each row as a list of strings.
Elements.Notes: All values read by csv.reader are strings; numeric data must be explicitly converted using int() or float() before mathematical operations.

Items.Text: Pass the open file object to csv.reader to create a reader object.
Iterate over the reader to process each row as a list.
Use next(reader) to skip a header row before processing data rows.

Elements.Title: Reading CSV Files with csv.DictReader
Elements.BodyText: csv.DictReader reads each row as an ordered dictionary, using the header row as keys, which makes column access by name more readable.
Elements.Notes: DictReader is particularly useful when CSVs have many columns and you only need a subset, since you can access values by column name rather than numeric index.

Items.Text: DictReader automatically uses the first row as field names unless fieldnames is specified.
Access row values by column name: row['Temperature'].
Each row is returned as a dictionary-like object with consistent key ordering.

Elements.Title: Writing CSV Files with csv.writer
Elements.BodyText: csv.writer provides methods to write rows to a CSV file, handling quoting and delimiter escaping automatically.
Elements.Notes: On Windows, open files for csv.writer with newline='' to prevent blank lines from being inserted between rows due to universal newline translation.

Items.Text: Create a writer object by passing the open file object to csv.writer.
Use writer.writerow(list) to write a single row.
Use writer.writerows(list_of_lists) to write multiple rows at once.

Elements.Title: Writing CSV Files with csv.DictWriter
Elements.BodyText: csv.DictWriter writes dictionaries as CSV rows, with field names specified at construction time.
Elements.Notes: Calling writer.writeheader() writes the fieldnames as the first row, ensuring the output file is self-describing and easy to re-import.

Items.Text: Pass fieldnames as a list when creating the DictWriter object.
Call writeheader() before writing data rows to include column labels.
Use writerow(dict) or writerows(list_of_dicts) to write data.

Topics.Title: Working with JSON
Topics.Summary: Introduces JSON as a human-readable data interchange format and demonstrates how Python's json module serializes and deserializes data for configuration files and APIs.

Elements.Title: What Is JSON?
Elements.BodyText: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, text-based format for representing structured data using key-value pairs, arrays, and nested objects.
Elements.Notes: JSON is the dominant format for web APIs and is increasingly used in engineering applications for configuration files and structured sensor data logs.

Items.Text: JSON supports six data types: strings, numbers, booleans, null, arrays, and objects.
JSON objects correspond closely to Python dictionaries; arrays correspond to lists.
JSON files are plain text and can be opened and inspected in any text editor.

Elements.Title: Parsing JSON with json.loads and json.load
Elements.BodyText: Python's json module converts a JSON string or file into native Python data structures using the loads and load functions.
Elements.Notes: json.loads (note the 's') operates on a string already in memory, while json.load reads directly from a file object — a common source of confusion for new users.

Items.Text: json.loads(string) parses a JSON-formatted string and returns a Python object.
json.load(file_object) reads and parses JSON directly from an open file.
JSON objects become Python dicts, arrays become lists, and JSON null becomes None.

Elements.Title: Serializing Data with json.dumps and json.dump
Elements.BodyText: The json module converts Python objects into JSON-formatted strings or writes them to a file using dumps and dump.
Elements.Notes: The indent parameter in json.dumps(data, indent=4) produces human-readable, pretty-printed JSON, which is useful when writing configuration or log files.

Items.Text: json.dumps(object) returns a JSON-formatted string from a Python object.
json.dump(object, file_object) writes JSON directly to an open file.
Python dicts, lists, strings, numbers, booleans, and None are natively serializable.

Elements.Title: JSON for Configuration Files
Elements.BodyText: Storing simulation parameters or tool settings in a JSON file allows engineers to modify inputs without editing source code.
Elements.Notes: A common pattern is to load a JSON config at the start of a script, validate required keys are present, and then pass values into functions as a dictionary.

Items.Text: Define default parameters in a .json file and load them at script startup.
Updating the config file changes script behavior without touching Python code.
Nested JSON objects allow hierarchical grouping of related parameters.

Elements.Title: Limitations of Python's json Module
Elements.BodyText: Not all Python objects can be directly serialized to JSON; certain types require custom handling or conversion before they can be encoded.
Elements.Notes: NumPy arrays and datetime objects are common non-serializable types in engineering scripts. Convert arrays to lists with .tolist() and datetimes to strings with .isoformat() before calling json.dump.

Items.Text: Sets, tuples (stored as arrays), and custom class instances are not natively JSON-serializable.
A custom JSONEncoder subclass can extend serialization to additional types.
Always validate JSON output when working with mixed Python and NumPy numeric types.

Topics.Title: Error Handling with try-except
Topics.Summary: Explains Python's exception handling model and demonstrates how to use try-except blocks to build resilient engineering scripts that recover gracefully from runtime errors.

Elements.Title: What Is an Exception?
Elements.BodyText: An exception is a runtime error that interrupts normal program flow; if not handled, it terminates the script and displays a traceback.
Elements.Notes: Common built-in exceptions in engineering scripts include FileNotFoundError, ValueError, ZeroDivisionError, TypeError, and KeyError. Understanding which exception to expect helps write targeted handlers.

Items.Text: Python raises an exception object when an error condition occurs.
An unhandled exception prints a traceback and stops execution immediately.
Exceptions are organized in a class hierarchy; catching a parent class also catches its subclasses.

Elements.Title: Basic try-except Structure
Elements.BodyText: A try block contains code that might raise an exception; the except block defines how to respond if a specific exception type is raised.
Elements.Notes: Catching the broad Exception base class is sometimes necessary but can hide unexpected bugs. Prefer catching the most specific exception type relevant to the operation.

Items.Text: Code inside the try block executes normally until an exception is raised.
If an exception matches the specified type, the except block executes instead of crashing.
Multiple except clauses can handle different exception types separately.

Elements.Title: The else and finally Clauses
Elements.BodyText: The optional else clause runs only if no exception was raised; the finally clause runs unconditionally and is used for cleanup actions.
Elements.Notes: finally is particularly valuable in scripts that open external resources such as network connections or file handles that must be released regardless of success or failure.

Items.Text: The else block executes when the try block completes without raising an exception.
The finally block always executes, making it ideal for releasing resources.
Both else and finally are optional and can be used together or independently.

Elements.Title: Accessing Exception Information
Elements.BodyText: Using 'as' in an except clause binds the exception object to a variable, providing access to its message and attributes for logging or debugging.
Elements.Notes: Printing or logging the exception object with str(e) or repr(e) gives the error message without a full traceback, which is appropriate for user-facing output in engineering tools.

Items.Text: Use 'except ExceptionType as e' to capture the exception instance.
str(e) returns the human-readable error message associated with the exception.
The exception object may carry additional attributes relevant to the specific error type.

Elements.Title: Raising Exceptions
Elements.BodyText: The raise statement lets you deliberately trigger an exception when your code detects an invalid condition, signaling errors clearly to calling code.
Elements.Notes: Raising a ValueError with a descriptive message when a function receives an out-of-range engineering parameter is far more informative than allowing the script to silently produce incorrect results.

Items.Text: Use 'raise ExceptionType(message)' to raise a specific exception with a description.
Re-raise a caught exception with a bare 'raise' statement to propagate it after logging.
Custom exception classes can be created by subclassing Exception for domain-specific errors.

Elements.Title: Practical Error Handling in Engineering Scripts
Elements.BodyText: Combining try-except with file I/O, user input, and numeric operations produces robust scripts that handle real-world data quality issues gracefully.
Elements.Notes: A common pattern is to wrap file reading and data parsing in a try-except block that logs the problematic row and continues processing, rather than aborting the entire run on a single bad data point.

Items.Text: Validate inputs at the boundary of your script using try-except before processing begins.
Log caught exceptions with enough context to identify the source of the problem.
Design error handling so the script can continue processing remaining data after a recoverable error.
